text: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.
section_title: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

text: This rule sets a threshold for what constitutes an offence. It states that an act cannot be considered an offence if the harm caused is so trivial that it would not be complained of by any reasonable person.
section_title: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

text: For example, if someone accidentally bumps into another person while walking and causes a minor bruise, this act is unlikely to be considered an offence under this rule as the harm caused would not be significant enough for most people to complain.
section_title: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.
